timeline.event: Helen Adams Keller was born in a house at Ivy Green in Tuscumbia, Alabama, USA.
Helen's mother, Kate Keller, was inspired by reading Charles Dickens' 'American Notes' and went to Baltimore to seek advice from J. Julian Chisolm.
Anne Sullivan arrived at Helen's house and began to teach her.
Helen enrolled in the Perkins School for the Blind.
Wrote 'The Frost King' at the age of 11.
Helen Keller and Anne Sullivan moved to New York to attend the Wright-Humason School for the Deaf and the Horace Mann School for the Deaf.
Returned to Massachusetts and attended The Cambridge School for Young Ladies.
Admitted to Radcliffe College, the women's college affiliated with Harvard University.
Published her autobiography 'The Story of My Life' with Anne Sullivan and Sullivan's husband, John Macy.
Graduated from Radcliffe College at the age of 24, becoming the first deafblind person to earn a Bachelor of Arts degree.
Wrote 'The World I Live In'.
Joined the Socialist Party of America at the age of 29.
Joined the Industrial Workers of the World (IWW).
Published 'Out of the Dark', a series of essays on socialism.
Founded an organization called 'Helen Keller International' with George Kessler.
Wrote articles for the Industrial Workers of the World (IWW).
Appeared in the silent film 'Deliverance'.
Helped found the American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU).
Published her spiritual autobiography 'My Religion'.
Anne Sullivan died.
Asked about the loyal dog Hachiko during a visit to Akita Prefecture, Japan, and received an Akita dog named 'Kamikaze-go'.
When 'Kamikaze-go' died, she received his older brother 'Kenzan-go' as an official gift from the Japanese government.
William Gibson's script for Playhouse 90 was realized as the first production of 'The Miracle Worker'.
Polly Thomson died of a stroke.
Suffered a series of strokes and spent her final years at home.
The film 'The Miracle Worker' starring Anne Bancroft and Patty Duke won Academy Awards.
Received the Presidential Medal of Freedom from US President Lyndon B. Johnson.
Inducted into the National Women's Hall of Fame at the New York World's Fair.
Died at the age of 88 at her home, Arcan Ridge, in Easton, Connecticut.
Helen Keller was selected as one of the 18 'most widely admired people of the 20th century' by Gallup.
The state of Alabama featured Helen Keller on its state quarter (25-cent coin).
A bronze statue of Helen Keller was added to the National Statuary Hall Collection.

keyAchievements.title: First deafblind person to earn a Bachelor of Arts degree
keyAchievements.description: Graduated from Radcliffe College in 1904, becoming the first blind and deaf person to earn a Bachelor of Arts degree.

keyAchievements.title: Prolific author and educator
keyAchievements.description: Published a total of 12 books and numerous articles, and was active as an American author and educator.

keyAchievements.title: Socialist and human rights activist
keyAchievements.description: Actively participated in various social movements including women's suffrage, pacifism, abolition of the death penalty, and opposition to child labor and racism.

keyAchievements.title: Founded Helen Keller International
keyAchievements.description: Co-founded 'Helen Keller International' with George Kessler in 1915, dedicated to research in vision, health, and nutrition.

keyAchievements.title: Contributed to the founding of the ACLU
keyAchievements.description: Helped found the American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU) in 1920.

keyAchievements.title: National honors
keyAchievements.description: Awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 1964, inducted into the National Women's Hall of Fame in 1965, and honored in various ways posthumously.

faq.question: When was Helen Keller born and when did she die?
faq.answer: Helen Keller was born on June 27, 1880, and died on June 1, 1968, at the age of 88.

faq.question: How did Helen Keller lose her sight and hearing?
faq.answer: At 19 months of age, she was diagnosed with scarlet fever and meningitis, which left her with lifelong visual and hearing impairments.

faq.question: Who had the greatest influence on Helen Keller's education?
faq.answer: Teacher Anne Sullivan arrived at Helen's house in March 1887 and had a decisive influence on her life by teaching her language by spelling words into her hand.

faq.question: What social movements did Helen Keller participate in?
faq.answer: Helen Keller was a socialist intellectual who practiced various human rights movements, including women's suffrage, pacifism, abolition of the death penalty, and opposition to child labor and racism.

faq.question: What is the famous work about Helen Keller's life?
faq.answer: The film 'The Miracle Worker', which depicts Helen Keller's childhood and her relationship with Anne Sullivan, is the most well-known, and the 1962 film won Academy Awards.

timeline.event: Baptized at the Catholic Parish of St. Remigius in Bonn, Germany.
Promoted as a six-year-old 'child prodigy' on the poster for his first public concert.
Began taking lessons from Christian Gottlob Neefe in Bonn.
Began working as Neefe's assistant organist.
His first published work, the piano variations 'Nine Variations on a March by Ernst Christoph Dressler, WoO 63', was published.
Three Piano Sonatas, WoO 47, were published.
The first article about Beethoven appeared in the 'Magazin der Musik'.
Began working as a paid employee at the court chapel and was permanently appointed as the second court organist.
His father Johann was forced to retire from the court, and an order was issued that half of his pension be paid directly to Beethoven to support the family.
Believed to have been commissioned to compose the 'Cantata on the Death of Emperor Joseph II, WoO 87' and the 'Cantata on the Accession of Emperor Leopold II, WoO 88'.
Believed to have been first introduced to Joseph Haydn when Haydn stopped in Bonn during his trip to London.
Commissioned to compose the ballet music 'Musik zu einem Ritterballett, WoO 1'.
Met Haydn again in Bonn on Haydn's return trip from London to Vienna, and Haydn agreed to take Beethoven as a student.
Left Bonn for Vienna with the help of the Elector.
Learned of his father's death shortly after arriving in Vienna.
Established a reputation as a piano virtuoso in Vienna.
Bonn fell to the French.
Performed one of his piano concertos at his first public concert in Vienna.
Arranged for the publication of his first work assigned an opus number (Three Piano Trios Nos. 1-3, Op. 1).
Toured cultural centers in Central Europe, including Prague, Dresden, Leipzig, and Berlin.
Composed Cello Sonatas Nos. 1-2, Op. 5 in Berlin and dedicated them to King Frederick William II.
Returned to Vienna.
Traveled to Pressburg and Pest.
Suffered from severe illness every summer and autumn.
First noticed problems with his hearing.
As his hearing gradually weakened during a trip to Prague, he eventually gave up concert tours altogether.
Composed his first six String Quartets, Op. 18 at the request of Prince Joseph Franz Lobkowitz.
Won a piano 'duel' against the virtuoso Joseph Woelfl.
Piano Sonata No. 8 'Pathétique', Op. 13 was published.
Completed the Septet, Op. 20.
Began teaching piano to the daughters of the Hungarian noblewoman Anna Brunsvik.
Won a piano 'duel' against Daniel Steibelt.
Hired the Burgtheater for the premiere of his first symphony and staged an extensive program.
With the premiere of Symphonies Nos. 1 and 2, he came to be regarded as the most important of a young generation of composers.
The first six String Quartets, Op. 18 were published and dedicated to Prince Lobkowitz.
Completed the ballet music 'The Creatures of Prometheus, Op. 43' and premiered it at the Burgtheater in Vienna.
Met the young countess Giulietta Guicciardi.
Taught Ferdinand Ries.
Young Carl Czerny studied under Beethoven.
Published Piano Sonata No. 14, Op. 27 No. 2 ('Moonlight Sonata') and dedicated it to Giulietta Guicciardi.
Completed Symphony No. 2, but the concert was canceled.
Moved to Heiligenstadt just outside Vienna to try and come to terms with his condition, and wrote the Heiligenstadt Testament.
Began studying piano and composition with Archduke Rudolf of Austria.
Composed Symphony No. 3 'Eroica'.
Count Joseph Deym died.
His position at the Theater an der Wien ended due to a change in management.
Disillusioned by Napoleon declaring himself 'Emperor', he scratched Napoleon's name off the title page of the manuscript of Symphony No. 3.
Symphony No. 3 'Eroica' was premiered.
The opera Fidelio was premiered to nearly empty houses due to the French occupation of the city.
Symphony No. 3 'Eroica' was published.
Musician and publisher Muzio Clementi secured the rights to publish his works in Britain.
Prince Esterhazy commissioned a mass (Mass in C major, Op. 86) for his wife's name day.
Organized and premiered a massive benefit concert that included Symphonies Nos. 5 and 6, Piano Concerto No. 4, the Mass in C major, and the Choral Fantasy.
Was offered the position of Kapellmeister at the court of Kassel by Jerome Bonaparte, leading Archduke Rudolf, Count Kinsky, and Prince Lobkowitz to pledge him a pension to stay in Vienna.
Commissioned to compose incidental music for Johann Wolfgang von Goethe's play Egmont.
The incidental music for Egmont (Op. 84) appeared.
Proposed marriage to Therese Malfatti but was rejected.
Prince Lobkowitz suspended his pension payments.
Fell seriously ill with headaches and high fever.
Met Goethe in Teplitz.
While in Teplitz, he wrote a 10-page love letter to his 'Immortal Beloved'.
Visited his younger brother Johann, hoping to end his cohabitation with Therese Obermayer.
His brother Johann and Therese Obermayer were married.
Prince Kinsky died after falling from a horse.
Went through a difficult emotional period and his compositional output dropped.
Lent 1,500 florins to his brother Kaspar.
Motivated to start composing by the news of Napoleon's defeat at the Battle of Vitoria by the coalition led by the Duke of Wellington.
Wellington's Victory (Op. 91) and his Seventh Symphony, Op. 92 were first performed at a charity concert for war victims.
Wellington's Victory was received with repeated performances.
Beethoven made his final public appearance as a soloist playing in the Archduke Trio.
Began using an ear trumpet designed by Maelzel.
Produced music with a patriotic tone to entertain the many heads of state and diplomats attending the Congress of Vienna.
Received compensation from Prince Lobkowitz by resorting to the law.
Composed 'Calm Sea and Prosperous Voyage', Op. 112, for choir and orchestra based on poems by Goethe.
After his brother Kaspar died, he became embroiled in a long legal battle with Kaspar's wife Johanna over the custody of his nephew Karl.
Beethoven's output dropped to a level unequaled in his mature life.
Succeeded in taking his nephew Karl away from his mother's custody.
Completed 'To the Distant Beloved', Op. 98.
Suffered from a prolonged illness ('inflammatory fever').
Sought and obtained Beethoven's endorsement for Maelzel's newly developed metronome.
Beethoven's health improved, and his eleven-year-old nephew Karl moved in with him in January.
Completed Piano Sonata No. 29 'Hammerklavier', Op. 106.
Began musical sketches that would become part of his final Symphony No. 9.
Lost sole guardianship when the case was moved to the Vienna magistracy.
It was announced that Archduke Rudolf was promoted to Cardinal-Archbishop.
Publisher Anton Diabelli invited 50 Viennese composers, including Beethoven, to compose a variation.
Completed twenty of what would become the thirty-three Diabelli Variations, Op. 120.
Publisher Moritz Schlesinger visited Beethoven for the first time.
Regained custody of his nephew Karl after a fierce legal battle.
Rudolf's installation in Olomouc (triggered the Missa solemnis, Op. 123).
Piano Sonata No. 30, Op. 109 was dispatched to the publisher.
Suffered from rheumatism and jaundice, showing poor health once again.
In a letter to Rudolf, Beethoven revealed his belief in a personal God.
Piano Sonata No. 31, Op. 110 was published.
Wished to reconcile with his married brother Johann.
'Calm Sea and Prosperous Voyage', Op. 112 was published with a dedication to the poet Goethe.
The Philharmonic Society of London proposed a commission for a symphony.
Prince Nikolai Galitzin requested three string quartets from Beethoven.
Anton Schindler began working as the composer's unpaid secretary.
Missa solemnis, Symphony No. 9, and Diabelli Variations were completed.
Presented a copy of the completed Mass to Rudolf.
Symphony No. 9 and sections of the Missa solemnis were performed for the first time at the Kärntnertortheater to great acclaim.
The second performance, for which the producer guaranteed a minimum fee, had poor attendance.
String Quartet No. 12 in E-flat major, Op. 127 was premiered by the Schuppanzigh Quartet.
Suffered a sudden illness while writing Quartet No. 15 in A minor, Op. 132.
The final contrapuntal movement of Quartet No. 13 in B-flat major, Op. 130 proved very difficult for both performers and audience at its premiere.
Despite reconciling with Schindler, his secretary was replaced by Karl Holz.
Nephew Karl, who had been seeing his mother again, attempted suicide.
Moved to his brother Johann's house in Gneixendorf with his nephew and stayed for three months.
Completed the substitute finale for Quartet No. 13 in B-flat major, Op. 130, his final work.
Caught pneumonia on the return journey to Vienna in an open carriage in cold and damp weather.
His nephew Karl left to join the army in Jihlava.
Said to Schindler and others, 'Applaud, friends, the comedy is over.'
Died at the age of 56.

keyAchievements.title: Activities as a composer and pianist
keyAchievements.description: A German composer of Western classical music and pianist, he is a major musician who was active during the transition between the Classical and Romantic eras.

keyAchievements.title: Moniker of 'Saint of Music'
keyAchievements.description: Often depicted as a heroic figure, he is called by the moniker 'Saint of Music' (Akseong).

keyAchievements.title: Major representative works
keyAchievements.description: His most widely known works include Symphony No. 3, Symphony No. 5, Symphony No. 6, Symphony No. 9, Pathétique Sonata, and Moonlight Sonata.

keyAchievements.title: First published work
keyAchievements.description: In March 1783, he published his first work, the piano variations 'Nine Variations on a March by Ernst Christoph Dressler, WoO 63'.

keyAchievements.title: Early piano sonatas
keyAchievements.description: The Three Piano Sonatas, WoO 47, published in 1783, were praised for giving an early glimpse of his talent and boldness as a composer.

keyAchievements.title: Ballet music commission
keyAchievements.description: In 1791, he was commissioned to compose the ballet music 'Musik zu einem Ritterballett, WoO 1', which was Beethoven's first work for the stage.

keyAchievements.title: First Opus work
keyAchievements.description: In 1795, he achieved financial success by publishing his first work assigned an opus number (Three Piano Trios Nos. 1-3, Op. 1).

keyAchievements.title: Cello Sonatas Nos. 1-2, Op. 5
keyAchievements.description: Composed in Berlin in 1796, they are famous for carefully considering the different characters of the cello and piano and combining the virtuoso parts well.

keyAchievements.title: Piano Sonata No. 8 'Pathétique', Op. 13
keyAchievements.description: Published in 1799, this work was described as surpassing any of his previous works 'in strength of character, depth of emotion, level of originality, and ingenuity of motif and tone'.

keyAchievements.title: Septet, Op. 20
keyAchievements.description: Completed in 1799, it was one of his most popular works during his lifetime.

keyAchievements.title: First six String Quartets, Op. 18
keyAchievements.description: Composed between 1798 and 1800, they were published in 1801 and dedicated to Prince Lobkowitz.

keyAchievements.title: Symphonies Nos. 1 and 2
keyAchievements.description: Premiered in 1800 and 1803, they led him to be regarded as the most important of the young generation of composers after Haydn and Mozart.

keyAchievements.title: Ballet music 'The Creatures of Prometheus, Op. 43'
keyAchievements.description: Completed in the spring of 1801, it premiered at the Burgtheater in Vienna and achieved success.

keyAchievements.title: Piano Sonata No. 14 'Moonlight Sonata', Op. 27 No. 2
keyAchievements.description: Published in 1802 and dedicated to Giulietta Guicciardi.

keyAchievements.title: Symphony No. 3 'Eroica'
keyAchievements.description: Composed in 1803-04, it is a major work that marks the beginning of his middle or 'heroic' period.

keyAchievements.title: Opera Fidelio
keyAchievements.description: Premiered in November 1805, it was later completed through revisions.

keyAchievements.title: Mass in C major, Op. 86
keyAchievements.description: Composed in 1807 commissioned by Prince Esterhazy.

keyAchievements.title: Massive benefit concert
keyAchievements.description: In December 1808, he organized and premiered a massive benefit concert that included Symphonies Nos. 5 and 6, Piano Concerto No. 4, the Mass in C major, and the Choral Fantasy.

keyAchievements.title: Incidental music for Goethe's play Egmont, Op. 84
keyAchievements.description: Commissioned in late 1809 and appeared in 1810, it fit well into Beethoven's 'heroic' style.

keyAchievements.title: String Quartet No. 11 in F minor, Op. 95 ('Quartetto Serioso')
keyAchievements.description: Known as another work from this period.

keyAchievements.title: Piano Trio No. 7 in B-flat major, Op. 97 ('Archduke Trio')
keyAchievements.description: Dedicated to his patron Rudolf.

keyAchievements.title: Wellington's Victory, Op. 91 and Symphony No. 7, Op. 92
keyAchievements.description: First performed at a charity concert on December 8, 1813, achieving great success.

keyAchievements.title: Piano Sonata No. 27 in E minor, Op. 90
keyAchievements.description: His first piano sonata in five years, composed in the summer of 1814.

keyAchievements.title: To the Distant Beloved, Op. 98
keyAchievements.description: Completed in 1816, it introduced the song cycle into the classical repertoire.

keyAchievements.title: Piano Sonata No. 29 'Hammerklavier', Op. 106
keyAchievements.description: One of the major works completed in 1818.

keyAchievements.title: Missa solemnis, Op. 123
keyAchievements.description: Triggered by the announcement of Archduke Rudolf's promotion in 1819, it was completed in 1823.

keyAchievements.title: Thirty-three Diabelli Variations, Op. 120
keyAchievements.description: Started at the request of Antonio Diabelli in 1819 and completed in 1823.

keyAchievements.title: Piano Sonatas No. 30, Op. 109 and No. 31, Op. 110
keyAchievements.description: Some of his final piano sonatas, published in 1820 and 1821 respectively.

keyAchievements.title: Symphony No. 9
keyAchievements.description: Starting sketches in 1818, it was completed in 1823 and premiered on May 7, 1824, receiving praise that 'the inexhaustible genius has shown us a new world'.

keyAchievements.title: String Quartet No. 12 in E-flat major, Op. 127
keyAchievements.description: Premiered by the Schuppanzigh Quartet in March 1825.

keyAchievements.title: Quartet No. 14 in C-sharp minor, Op. 131
keyAchievements.description: The final part of the series, evaluated by Beethoven as his most perfect single work.

keyAchievements.title: Quartet No. 16 in F major, Op. 135
keyAchievements.description: An additional quartet completed while staying in Gneixendorf.

faq.question: When and where was Beethoven born?
faq.answer: He was born in Bonn, Germany, and there is a record of his baptism on December 17, 1770. Since it was customary at the time to baptize within 24 hours of birth, Beethoven himself considered December 16 as his birthday.

faq.question: What are Beethoven's major works?
faq.answer: His most widely known works include Symphony No. 3, Symphony No. 5, Symphony No. 6, Symphony No. 9, Pathétique Sonata, and Moonlight Sonata.

faq.question: When did Beethoven die, and what is suspected to be the cause of his death?
faq.answer: He died on March 26, 1827, at the age of 56. Liver cirrhosis, complications from pneumonia, and lead poisoning are suspected as causes of death.

faq.question: Did Beethoven completely lose his hearing?
faq.answer: Beethoven gradually lost his hearing, but even in his later years he could distinguish low tones and sudden loud noises, and did not become completely deaf.

faq.question: What is Beethoven's moniker?
faq.answer: He is often depicted as a heroic figure and is also called by the moniker 'Saint of Music' (Akseong).

faq.question: During which musical period was Beethoven active?
faq.answer: He is a major musician who was active during the transition between the Classical and Romantic eras.

timeline.event: Born in Hyde Park, New York.
His father, James Roosevelt I, died.
Graduated from Harvard University in 3 years.
Enrolled in Columbia Law School.
Married Eleanor Roosevelt.
Passed the New York bar exam and dropped out of Columbia Law School.
Elected to the New York State Senate.
Inaugurated as New York State Senator.
Re-elected to the New York State Senate.
Appointed Assistant Secretary of the Navy under Woodrow Wilson.
Eleanor Roosevelt discovered his affair with Lucy Mercer.
Ran as James M. Cox's vice presidential running mate but was defeated.
His lower body became paralyzed due to illness and was diagnosed with polio.
Established a rehabilitation center in Warm Springs, Georgia.
Elected Governor of New York.
The Wall Street Crash occurred and the Great Depression began.
Re-elected as Governor of New York.
Won the US presidential election against Herbert Hoover.
Survived an assassination attempt by Giuseppe Zangara.
Inaugurated as the 32nd President of the United States.
Declared a four-day 'bank holiday'.
The Emergency Banking Act was passed.
Signed the Cullen-Harrison Act, ending Prohibition.
Implemented the National Industrial Recovery Act (NIRA).
Reformed financial regulations through the Glass-Steagall Act.
Established the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC).
Established the Federal Communications Commission (FCC).
The US Supreme Court declared the National Industrial Recovery Act unconstitutional.
The Social Security Act was passed.
Established the Works Progress Administration (WPA).
Re-elected in a landslide victory in the US presidential election.
Failed to pass the Judicial Procedures Reform Bill.
The Housing Act was passed.
Founded the National Foundation for Infantile Paralysis.
The Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA) was passed.
Passed the Reorganization Act and established the Executive Office of the President.
World War II broke out with Germany's invasion of Poland.
Won the US presidential election, becoming the only president to serve more than three terms.
Delivered the 'Four Freedoms' speech.
Obtained congressional approval for the Lend-Lease Act.
Received a declaration of war from Japan following the attack on Pearl Harbor.
Germany and Italy declared war on the United States.
Died.

keyAchievements.title: Longest-serving US President
keyAchievements.description: He is the only president in US history to serve the longest and complete more than three terms.

keyAchievements.title: Implementation of the New Deal
keyAchievements.description: Implemented the New Deal policies to overcome the Great Depression, providing relief to the unemployed and farmers, and seeking economic recovery.

keyAchievements.title: Political realignment and pioneering modern liberalism
keyAchievements.description: Built the 'New Deal Coalition', reorganizing US politics into the Fifth Party System and pioneering modern liberalism.

keyAchievements.title: Financial and labor reforms
keyAchievements.description: Introduced major regulatory reforms in finance, communications, and labor, such as establishing the SEC and the FDIC, and enacting the National Labor Relations Act.

keyAchievements.title: Repeal of Prohibition
keyAchievements.description: Signed the Cullen-Harrison Act to end the Prohibition era.

keyAchievements.title: Leadership during World War II
keyAchievements.description: Worked closely with other national leaders to lead the Allied Powers against the Axis Powers and oversaw the economic mobilization of the United States.

keyAchievements.title: Initiation of nuclear weapon development
keyAchievements.description: Initiated the development of the world's first nuclear weapons.

keyAchievements.title: Laying the foundation for the United Nations
keyAchievements.description: Cooperated with other Allied leaders to lay the foundation for the United Nations and other postwar institutions.

keyAchievements.title: Promotion of the Good Neighbor Policy
keyAchievements.description: Promoted the Good Neighbor Policy, renouncing the right to unilaterally intervene in the affairs of Latin American countries.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Social Security System
keyAchievements.description: Legally established the Social Security System through the Social Security Act, promising economic security to the elderly, the poor, and the disabled.

keyAchievements.title: Improvement of labor standards
keyAchievements.description: Passed the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA), outlawing child labor and establishing a federal minimum wage.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Executive Office of the President
keyAchievements.description: Established the Executive Office of the President, the backbone of the federal executive branch, through the Reorganization Act of 1939.

keyAchievements.title: Environmental conservation efforts
keyAchievements.description: Encouraged the expansion and funding of national parks and national forests and made environmental conservation efforts through the Civilian Conservation Corps.

faq.question: Which president was Franklin D. Roosevelt?
faq.answer: He served as the 32nd President of the United States.

faq.question: What are the major achievements of Franklin D. Roosevelt?
faq.answer: He implemented the New Deal to overcome the Great Depression and led the Allies in World War II.

faq.question: How many times was Franklin D. Roosevelt elected president?
faq.answer: He was elected president a total of 4 times, the only person in US history to serve more than 3 terms.

faq.question: What disease did Franklin D. Roosevelt suffer from?
faq.answer: He was diagnosed with polio in 1921, which left his lower body paralyzed.

faq.question: Who was Franklin D. Roosevelt's wife?
faq.answer: His wife was Eleanor Roosevelt.

faq.question: When did Franklin D. Roosevelt die?
faq.answer: He died on April 12, 1945.

timeline.event: Born as Marcus Aurelius Antoninus in Rome.
Adopted the dress and lifestyle of a philosopher on the orders of Diognetus.
Adopted by Hadrian's successor, Antoninus Pius.
Antoninus Pius ascended to the throne as emperor following Hadrian's death.
Served as consul under Antoninus Pius.
Became consul with Antoninus Pius as his co-consul.
Became consul for the second time.
Married Faustina, the daughter of Antoninus Pius.
Birth of his first daughter, Domitia Faustina.
Antoninus Pius granted Marcus tribunician power and imperium.
Faustina gave birth to twin brothers Titus Aurelius Antoninus and Tiberius Aelius Aurelius (who did not live long).
Birth of daughter Annia Aurelia Galeria Lucilla.
Estimated death of daughter Domitia Faustina.
Birth of son Tiberius Aelius Antoninus (who did not live long).
Death of Marcus's mother, Domitia Lucilla.
Faced another death of a child (name unknown).
Faustina gave birth to daughters Fadilla and Cornificia.
Following the death of Antoninus Pius, Marcus and Lucius Verus ascended as co-emperors.
Lowered the silver purity of the Roman denarius from 83.5% to 79%.
Vologases IV of Parthia entered the Kingdom of Armenia, expelled its king, and installed Pacorus.
Outbreak of the Parthian War.
Lucius left for the battlefield to personally handle the Parthian War.
Artaxata, the capital of Armenia, was captured.
Lucius gained the title 'Armeniacus'.
A new Armenian capital, Kaine Polis, replaced Artaxata.
The Roman army moved into Mesopotamia, captured Edessa, and reinstalled King Mannus. Nisibis fell.
Cassius's army reached Seleucia and Ctesiphon, captured Ctesiphon, burned the royal palace, and sacked Seleucia.
The Antonine Plague broke out in Mesopotamia and spread to Rome.
Cassius's army crossed the Tigris and headed into Media, returning to the battlefield. Lucius claimed the title 'Medicus'.
Marcus proclaimed his twin sons Annius and Commodus as heirs.
War broke out with Germanic tribes.
Revalued the denarius by increasing its silver purity from 79% to 82%.
Co-emperor Lucius likely died of the plague.
The Roman army routed the Germanic tribes in the province of Dacia, but commander Fronto was killed in action.
Moors in the North African provinces rebelled and invaded as far as Hispania.
Began co-ruling with his son Commodus.
Died at the age of 58 in a military camp near Vindobona or Sirmium.

keyAchievements.title: Emperor of the Roman Empire
keyAchievements.description: He was the 16th emperor of the Roman Empire (161-180), the last of the Five Good Emperors, and the last emperor of the Pax Romana.

keyAchievements.title: Stoic Philosopher
keyAchievements.description: As a Stoic philosopher, he wrote 'Meditations', leaving important material for understanding ancient Stoic philosophy.

keyAchievements.title: Military victories
keyAchievements.description: Successfully fought wars in the East against the Parthian Empire and the Kingdom of Armenia, and subdued Germanic tribes in the Marcomannic Wars.

keyAchievements.title: Monetary reform
keyAchievements.description: Took measures to correct the silver purity of the Roman coin, the denarius.

keyAchievements.title: Legal and administrative activities
keyAchievements.description: Showed significant interest in three areas: the manumission of slaves, the protection of orphans and minors, and the choice of city councilors.

keyAchievements.title: Erection of monuments
keyAchievements.description: The Column of Marcus Aurelius and his equestrian statue were erected in Rome to commemorate his military victories.

faq.question: Who was Marcus Aurelius?
faq.answer: He was the 16th emperor of the Roman Empire (161-180) and a Stoic philosopher, being the last of the Five Good Emperors and the last emperor of the Pax Romana.

faq.question: What were his major military achievements?
faq.answer: He successfully fought wars in the East against the Parthian Empire and the Kingdom of Armenia, and subdued the Marcomanni, Quadi, and the Sarmatian Iazyges in the Marcomannic Wars.

faq.question: What is the important work he wrote?
faq.answer: The 'Meditations' he wrote is an important source for understanding ancient Stoic philosophy today and provides a window into his inner life.

faq.question: What difficulties did the Roman Empire face during his reign?
faq.answer: During his reign, the Roman Empire faced massive military conflicts, and the Antonine Plague that broke out in 165 or 166 devastated the Roman Empire's population, causing 5 million deaths out of a population of 10 million.

faq.question: How did Marcus Aurelius choose his successor?
faq.answer: He chose not to adopt an heir, and his son Commodus succeeded him as emperor, which was a subject of debate among both contemporary and modern historians.

timeline.event: Born to his father Shu Lianghe and mother Yan Zhengzai in Zou, Changping, Qufu in the State of Lu.
His father, Shu Lianghe, died.
Married.
His mother, Yan Zhengzai, died.
Married the daughter of the Bingguan family of the State of Song.
Had a son, Li (鯉).
Opened an academy in the State of Lu and began teaching students.
Started his career as an official, passing through positions such as Weili (accountant) and Sizhi (manager of pastures).
Fled to the State of Qi following Duke Zhao of Lu due to the 'Rebellion of the Three Huan' in the State of Lu.
Returned from the State of Qi.
Became Zhongduzai (magistrate of Zhongdu).
Rose to the position of Da Sikou (Minister of Crime).
Earned the enmity of Ji Sunsi, left the State of Lu, and wandered through various states for 14 years.
Suffered grief as he successively lost his son Boyu and his disciples Yan Hui and Zilu.
Returned to his hometown of Qufu and devoted himself to cultivating younger scholars.
Passed away with his disciples watching over him.
Emperor Xuanzong of Tang posthumously honored Confucius as 'King Wenxuan'.
Emperor Zhenzong of Song posthumously titled him 'Zhisheng Wenxuan Wang' (The Most Sage King Wenxuan).
Came to be called 'Dacheng Zhisheng Wenxuan Wang' (The Great, Supreme Sage and King Wenxuan).
Awarded the title 'Zhisheng Xianshi' (The Most Sage and Ancient Teacher).
Awarded the title 'Dacheng Zhisheng Wenxuan Xianshi' (The Great, Supreme Sage and Ancient Teacher Wenxuan).

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of China's first school
keyAchievements.description: Established an academy for the first time in Chinese history and taught students.

keyAchievements.title: Abolition of the evil practice of human sacrifice
keyAchievements.description: Advised the king and abolished the evil practice of human sacrifice (sati) which had continued until then.

keyAchievements.title: Recovery of the lost territory of the State of Lu
keyAchievements.description: Recovered the old territory that the State of Lu had lost through brilliant strategies in negotiations with the State of Qi.

keyAchievements.title: Education on the Six Classics
keyAchievements.description: Educated his disciples using the Six Classics as textbooks: the 'Book of Changes', 'Book of Odes', 'Book of Documents', 'Book of Rites', 'Classic of Music', and 'Spring and Autumn Annals'.

keyAchievements.title: Advocacy of the philosophy of Ren (Benevolence)
keyAchievements.description: Advocated humanism, placing 'Ren' (Benevolence) as the ultimate orientation of human behavior.

keyAchievements.title: Cultivating younger scholars
keyAchievements.description: In his later years, he returned to his hometown and devoted himself to cultivating younger scholars, producing many outstanding disciples.

faq.question: Who was Confucius?
faq.answer: He was a politician, philosopher, and educator in the Spring and Autumn period of ancient China, and is known as the founder of Confucianism.

faq.question: What is the core philosophy of Confucius?
faq.answer: Contained in the 'Analects', a collection of his words and deeds, he advocated humanism, placing the ultimate orientation of human behavior on 'Ren' (Benevolence).

faq.question: What educational activities did Confucius engage in?
faq.answer: He established an academy for the first time in Chinese history and strove to cultivate younger scholars by teaching classics such as the Book of Odes, the Book of Documents, and the Book of Changes.

faq.question: What was Confucius's political ideal?
faq.answer: He advocated rule by virtue, governing the people and the country with virtue rather than law, and humanism, which places the highest value on people.

faq.question: When did Confucius die?
faq.answer: He passed away in 479 BC at the age of 72, with his disciples watching over him.

timeline.event: Born in Athens, ancient Greece.
Participated directly in battles as a hoplite in three wars.
Strove to educate and reform the youth as an educator.
Participated in politics for one year as a member of the Council of 500.
Indicted on charges of 'impiety' and 'corrupting the youth', and executed by drinking hemlock at the age of 71.

keyAchievements.title: The first figure in Western philosophy
keyAchievements.description: Consistently devoted his life to discussing problems of philosophy, and is regarded as the first figure in Western philosophy.

keyAchievements.title: Development of the Maieutic method (Socratic method)
keyAchievements.description: Utilizing Zeno's dialectic, he developed the maieutic method, which awakens one to the contradictions of false judgments and guides them to correct ones.

keyAchievements.title: Emphasis on the connection between virtue and ignorance
keyAchievements.description: Emphasized the ethical aspect, viewing vice as stemming from ignorance and virtue as arising from the foundation of rational thinking.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of soul-centric thinking
keyAchievements.description: As a body-soul dualist, he argued that the soul is immortal and all knowledge is innate within the soul.

keyAchievements.title: Called the 'Greatest Teacher of Mankind'
keyAchievements.description: Influenced countless people with his outstanding intellectual and moral character, earning him the title of the 'Greatest Teacher of Mankind'.

faq.question: Did Socrates leave behind any writings?
faq.answer: Socrates did not leave behind any writings, and his thoughts have been passed down through the records of his disciples and contemporaries.

faq.question: Did Socrates say 'A bad law is still a law'?
faq.answer: There is no evidence that Socrates directly said 'A bad law is still a law'; this aphorism first appeared in the Roman era by Domitius Ulpianus.

faq.question: How did Socrates meet his death?
faq.answer: He was indicted on charges of 'impiety' and 'corrupting the youth', sentenced to death, and met his end by drinking a cup of poison (hemlock).

timeline.event: Born in Stagira in the region of Thrace on the Chalcidice peninsula in northern Greece.
Entered Plato's Academy at the age of 17.
Went to Atarneus at the invitation of the tyrant Hermias shortly before Plato's death.
Went to Mieza as a tutor to Prince Alexander at the request of King Philip II of Macedonia.
Retreated to Stagira and continued his research life when Alexander became regent.
Returned to Athens again, founded the Lyceum school, and spent 12 years lecturing and researching.
Fled to Chalcis, his mother's hometown, to escape anti-Macedonian sentiment following news of Alexander the Great's death.
Ended his 63-year life due to a stomach ailment.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of the Western philosophical system
keyAchievements.description: Created the first comprehensive system of Western philosophy, encompassing morality and aesthetics, logic and science, politics and metaphysics.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Lyceum school library and zoo
keyAchievements.description: Built a library at the Lyceum school with the help of Alexander the Great, and also created the world's first zoo.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of logic
keyAchievements.description: Gained a pioneering position in formal logic with the compilation of 'Prior Analytics', and invented the syllogism.

keyAchievements.title: Zoological research
keyAchievements.description: Observed, recorded, and classified over 500 species of animals, leaving precise records such as the discovery that whales and sharks give birth to live young.

keyAchievements.title: Proposal of the theory of the four causes
keyAchievements.description: Proposed the theory of the four causes, which explains the causes of moving and changing sensory objects from four aspects: material cause, formal cause, efficient cause, and final cause.

keyAchievements.title: Description of the camera obscura
keyAchievements.description: His book 'Problems' contains the oldest surviving description of the camera obscura, which corresponds to early cameras.

faq.question: Whose disciple and whose teacher was Aristotle?
faq.answer: Aristotle was a disciple of Plato and the teacher of Alexander the Great.

faq.question: What fields did Aristotle write books about?
faq.answer: He wrote books on a wide range of subjects including physics, metaphysics, poetry, biology, zoology, logic, rhetoric, politics, ethics, and moral science.

faq.question: What is the most famous example in Aristotle's logic?
faq.answer: The most famous example in Aristotle's logic is the 'syllogism', an argument based on deductive reasoning.

faq.question: What is Aristotle's theory of the four elements?
faq.answer: Aristotle proposed four elements: hot and dry fire, cold and dry earth, hot and wet air, and cold and wet water.

faq.question: What did Aristotle see as the purpose of education?
faq.answer: Aristotle placed the purpose of education on leading a rational life and achieving a happy life.

faq.question: What was Aristotle's view on women?
faq.answer: Aristotle's view on women had a profound influence on Western thinkers until the late Middle Ages and is treated as an important subject in women's history.

timeline.event: Birth.
Went to Sicily at the earnest request of Dionysius II to try and implement ideal politics.
Death.
The final year that the Academy founded by Plato continued.

keyAchievements.title: Founding of objective idealism
keyAchievements.description: A Greek philosopher and thinker who founded objective idealism.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of and teaching at the Academy
keyAchievements.description: Founded and taught at the 'Academy', a higher education institution that was the prototype for a university.

keyAchievements.title: Authorship of dialogues
keyAchievements.description: Wrote dialogues on many philosophical topics including politics, ethics, metaphysics, and epistemology.

keyAchievements.title: Advocacy of the Theory of Ideas (Forms)
keyAchievements.description: Became independent from the thoughts of his teacher Socrates and advocated his own doctrine called the Theory of Ideas.

keyAchievements.title: Development of the Philosopher King theory
keyAchievements.description: Developed the famous Philosopher King theory that 'philosophers must become kings, or kings must philosophize'.

keyAchievements.title: Founding the basis of early ideal communism
keyAchievements.description: Founded the basis of early ideal communism through a teleological view of humanity as beings endowed with the 'duty' to become 'rational beings' to build a better society.

faq.question: Who was Plato?
faq.answer: Plato was a disciple of Socrates, the teacher of Aristotle, and an influential Greek philosopher and thinker who founded objective idealism.

faq.question: What are Plato's main achievements?
faq.answer: He founded objective idealism, established the 'Academy' which was the prototype for a university, and advocated the Theory of Ideas and the Philosopher King theory.

faq.question: What are the characteristics of Plato's writings?
faq.answer: Most of his extant works take the form of dialogues, featuring his teacher Socrates as the main interlocutor.

faq.question: What was Plato's view on education?
faq.answer: Plato divided education into 5 stages and aimed to cultivate rulers by teaching literature, physical education, mathematics, and dialectics at each stage.

faq.question: What was Plato's view on child development?
faq.answer: Plato believed that human development had three aspects: desire, spirit, and divine; he emphasized the importance of early childhood experiences and advocated for gender separation at age 6.

timeline.event: Born in the mountain village of Vinci in the Tuscany region.
According to tax records, he was living in the same house as his father.
His stepmother Albiera died.
At the age of 14, moved to Florence with his family and entered the workshop of Andrea del Verrocchio.
Registered with the painters' guild of Florence and became known as a painter.
Assisted in his master Verrocchio's work 'The Baptism of Christ' and painted the angel on the left side.
Invited as a painter by the noble Sforza family of Milan.
Produced 'The Virgin of the Rocks' (Louvre Museum).
Immersed himself in anatomical studies of the human body and horses, and devoted himself to treatises on human anatomy and the flight of birds, as well as research on optics.
Produced 'The Last Supper'.
Returned to Florence in 1500 due to the invasion of Milan by King Louis XII of France.
Invited by Cesare Borgia for civil engineering projects, canal development, and urban planning.
Returned to Florence and began painting the 'Mona Lisa'.
His father Piero died at the age of 77.
Became the court painter for King Louis XII of France in Milan.
Returned to Florence, then moved to Rome under the patronage of Giuliano de' Medici.
Met Francis I in Bologna and was invited to France.
His patron Giuliano de' Medici died.
Crossed the Alps and headed to Amboise, France, with his disciple Francesco Melzi.
Drafted his will as if sensing his death.
Died at the age of 67 at his residence in Clos Lucé, France.
With the death of Francesco Melzi, Leonardo da Vinci's massive collection of sketches and drawings was revealed to the world.
His remains were destroyed and no longer exist, as church buildings were dismantled during the French Revolution.

keyAchievements.title: A polymath in multiple fields
keyAchievements.description: A representative Italian polymath who was active in various fields including as a painter, sculptor, inventor, architect, anatomist, geographer, and musician.

keyAchievements.title: Selected as the most creative person
keyAchievements.description: Ranked first as the most creative person among the 10 geniuses who changed human history selected by the journal Nature in 2007.

keyAchievements.title: Mona Lisa
keyAchievements.description: The most famous and widely known painting in the world today, representing the Louvre Museum.

keyAchievements.title: The Last Supper
keyAchievements.description: One of his masterpieces, counted as one of the great paintings of the century.

keyAchievements.title: Development of the Sfumato technique
keyAchievements.description: Developed sfumato, an atmospheric perspective technique that paints even the air surrounding an object to create a completely three-dimensional world.

keyAchievements.title: Scientific discoveries and research
keyAchievements.description: Discovered the theory of wave motion, fluid pressure, water pumps, and hydraulic pressure, and researched various scientific fields such as flight principles, aerodynamics, anatomy, and botany.

keyAchievements.title: Book 'Treatise on Painting'
keyAchievements.description: Left behind many treatises, including 'Treatise on Painting', an important document of Renaissance art theory regarding painting.

faq.question: When and where was Leonardo da Vinci born?
faq.answer: Leonardo da Vinci was born at 3 AM on Saturday, April 15, 1452, in the mountain village of Vinci in the Tuscany region.

faq.question: What are Leonardo da Vinci's representative works?
faq.answer: Among his many works, the 'Mona Lisa' is the most famous today, and 'The Last Supper' is also counted as one of his masterpieces.

faq.question: In what fields did Leonardo da Vinci work?
faq.answer: He worked in and held various professions across many fields, including as a painter, sculptor, inventor, architect, anatomist, geographer, and musician.

faq.question: Did Leonardo da Vinci receive formal education?
faq.answer: Because he was illegitimate, he did not learn to read and write in a formal school, and he did not receive normal education in various subjects such as Latin and Greek.

faq.question: Where did Leonardo da Vinci die?
faq.answer: On May 2, 1519, he died at the age of 67 at his residence in Clos Lucé, France.

faq.question: Do Leonardo da Vinci's remains still exist today?
faq.answer: During the chaotic period of the French Revolution in 1802, church buildings were dismantled and tombs were vandalized, so his remains do not exist today.

faq.question: Did Leonardo da Vinci marry?
faq.answer: Leonardo da Vinci remained single his whole life and left no children.

timeline.event: Born in Salzburg, Austria.
Proficient in playing the keyboard and violin, and began composing.
Traveled on concert tours to various parts of Europe, including Germany, Paris, and London.
Studied composition under Johann Christian Bach in London, England.
Studied music theory under Giovanni Battista Martini during a trip to Italy.
Received decisive stimulation from the music of Haydn, Wagenseil, Monn, etc. during a trip to Vienna.
Premiered the Piano Concerto (K.242) with an instrument provided by Stein.
Was dismissed from his position as a court musician in Salzburg, left for Vienna, and stayed there until his death.
Married Constanze.
Joined a Masonic 'Charity' lodge.
Passed away.
His wife Constanze remarried the Danish diplomat Georg Nikolaus von Nissen.

keyAchievements.title: Composing a multitude of works
keyAchievements.description: Despite his short life, his rapid pace of creation left behind over 800 works across almost all genres of Western classical music at the time.

keyAchievements.title: Pinnacle works by genre
keyAchievements.description: Many of his works are considered the pinnacle of the symphony, concerto, chamber music, opera, and choral repertoire.

keyAchievements.title: Composition of major operas
keyAchievements.description: Composed operas such as 'Idomeneo', 'The Abduction from the Seraglio', 'The Marriage of Figaro', 'Don Giovanni', 'Così fan tutte', and 'The Magic Flute'.

keyAchievements.title: Piano concertos
keyAchievements.description: Composed more than 12 piano concertos in Vienna, which are considered some of his greatest achievements.

keyAchievements.title: Composition of masterpieces
keyAchievements.description: Composed masterpieces such as his last three symphonies including Symphony No. 41, the serenade 'Eine kleine Nachtmusik', the Clarinet Concerto, and the unfinished 'Requiem'.

faq.question: What was the cause of Mozart's death?
faq.answer: The records cite 'severe miliary fever', but there is insufficient information to diagnose it with modern medicine, and there are various theories such as trichinosis, poisoning, and rheumatic fever.

faq.question: Did Mozart die in poverty?
faq.answer: It is a common story that Mozart died in poverty and obscurity, but arguments that he had a reasonably satisfactory income are also persuasive.

faq.question: Did Mozart meet Beethoven?
faq.answer: An anecdote says they met in Vienna in 1784, but its reliability is not widely accepted today as the sole source of this story is Otto Jahn's biography of Mozart.

faq.question: Is it true that Mozart was poisoned by Antonio Salieri?
faq.answer: The story of the rivalry and poisoning between Mozart and Salieri is a legend that has been the subject of various works, including Alexander Pushkin's play, Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov's opera, and Peter Shaffer's play.

faq.question: Did Mozart compose everything at once by inspiration?
faq.answer: It is a legend that Mozart completed the music in his head by inspiration and then wrote it down without a single revision; there is an argument that he was actually a careful and hardworking composer.
